en.extract:
The Bornean swiftlet (Collocalia dodgei), also referred to as the Kinabalu 'linchi' swiftlet, is a small bird in the swift family Apodidae. It is endemic to the northern mountains of the island of Borneo. It has variously been considered a subspecies of the glossy swiftlet or the cave swiftlet, and in 2008 was split from the latter (C. linchi) as a full species C. dodgei.


Taxonomy
The Bornean swiftlet was described by the American ornithologist Charles Richmond in 1905 under current binomial name Collocalia dodgei. The specific epithet dodgei is in honour of H. D. Dodge who had collected the specimen on Mount Kinabalu in Borneo.


Description
At about 10 cm in length it is a tiny swiftlet with relatively short wings and a square tail. Mainly glossy dark grey to black in colour, it has a white belly. Compared with the glossy swiftlet, which also occurs within its range, it is slightly smaller, with a greenish, rather than bluish, gloss to the feathers, and with no white tail spots; nor does it have feathered hind toes.


Distribution and habitat
It is seen mainly around the massif of Mount Kinabalu, though there is a record from the Maligan Range of south-central Sabah. It has been found nesting on a hut at 2370 m above sea level on Kinabalu.


References

The '''Bornean swiftlet''' ('''''Collocalia dodgei'''''), also referred to as the '''Kinabalu 'linchi' swiftlet''', is a small [[bird]] in the [[Swift (bird)|swift]] [[family (biology)|family]] [[Apodidae]]. It is [[endemism|endemic]] to the northern mountains of the island of [[Borneo]].<ref name=p&p>{{cite book |title=Phillipps' Field Guide to the Birds of Borneo |author1=Phillipps, Quentin |author2=Phillipps, Karen |year=2011 |publisher=John Beaufoy Publishing |location=Oxford, UK |isbn=978-1-906780-56-2 }}</ref> It has variously been considered a subspecies of the [[glossy swiftlet]] or the [[cave swiftlet]], and in 2008 was split from the latter (''C. linchi'') as a full species ''C. dodgei''.<ref>{{cite web |url=http://www.birdlife.org |title=Collocalia linchi |access-date=2013-11-13 |work=Species factsheet |publisher=BirdLife International |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070710124603/http://www.birdlife.org/ |archive-date=2007-07-10 }}</ref><ref>{{cite journal |vauthors=Moyle RG, Hosner PA, Nais J, Lakim M, Sheldon FH |year= 2008|title= Taxonomic status of the Kinabalu 'linchi' swiftlet |journal= Bulletin of the British Ornithologists' Club |volume= 128|pages= 94–100}}</ref>

==Taxonomy==
The Bornean swiftlet was [[Species description|described]] by the American ornithologist [[Charles Wallace Richmond|Charles Richmond]] in 1905 under current [[binomial name]] ''Collocalia dodgei''. The specific epithet ''dodgei'' is in honour of H. D. Dodge who had collected the specimen on [[Mount Kinabalu]] in Borneo.<ref>{{ cite journal | last=Richmond | first=Charles Wallace | author-link=Charles Wallace Richmond | year=1905 | title=Description of a new swiftlet from Mount Kina Balu, Borneo | journal=Smithsonian Miscellaneous Collections | volume=47 | pages=431–432 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/8812229 }}</ref><ref>{{ cite book | editor-last=Peters | editor-first=James Lee | editor-link=James L. Peters | year=1940 | title=Check-list of Birds of the World | volume=4 | publisher=Harvard University Press | place=Cambridge, Massachusetts  | page=230 | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/14476701 }}</ref>

==Description==
At about 10 cm in length it is a tiny swiftlet with relatively short wings and a square tail.<ref>{{cite book |title= Handbook of the Birds of the World, vol. 5: Barn-owls to Hummingbirds |author1=del Hoyo, J. |author2=Elliott, A. |author3=Sargatal, J. |year= 1999|publisher= Lynx Edicions |location= Barcelona, Spain }}</ref> Mainly glossy dark grey to black in colour, it has a white belly. Compared with the glossy swiftlet, which also occurs within its range, it is slightly smaller, with a greenish, rather than bluish, gloss to the feathers, and with no white tail spots; nor does it have feathered hind toes.<ref name=p&p/>

==Distribution and habitat==
It is seen mainly around the massif of [[Mount Kinabalu]], though there is a record from the Maligan Range of south-central [[Sabah]]. It has been found nesting on a hut at 2370 m above sea level on Kinabalu.<ref name=p&p/>
